original_abstract: The failure to fulfill state responsibilities toward displaced and abandoned children stems primarily from a lack of institutionalized good governance and a deficit of political will within the government. In the Indonesian context, the protection of children's rights faces several systemic hurdles. Key among these is the inconsistent enforcement of existing laws, often hampered by limited capacity among legal officials and a lack of necessary infrastructure. Economic constraints further diminish the effectiveness of social programs, while a prevailing lack of awareness—both among parents and the broader public—regarding child rights and the mandates of the International Convention on the Rights of the Child exacerbates the issue. Furthermore, progress is stalled by fragmented coordination between government bodies and social organizations, as well as a lack of robust cross-sectoral and international cooperation. This study highlights the urgent need for structural reform and enhanced public-private synergy to secure the welfare of vulnerable children.
humanized_abstract: Failure to meet State responsibility towards detained and abandoned children is a function of lack of adequate institutional mechanism of good governance and the will of government institutions. Indonesia has a number of structural challenges which undermine the protection of children's rights as enshrined in laws and as is provided for in the International Convention on the Rights of the Child. Effective application of laws and regulations has been hindered due to weakness in the capacities of law enforcing officers as well as absence of necessary field infrastructure. In addition, social development programmes are constrained by financial difficulties as well as poor awareness of children rights and the application of the Convention on the Rights of the Child amongst parents and community members. Co-ordination between governmental institutions and non-governmental organizations is inefficient, whilst cross-sectoral and international cooperation has not yet been strengthened. The study found that a series of policy reforms and strengthening of partnerships between public and private organizations are needed to address the protection of neglected children in Indonesia.
